sourceMovie.title: Giant
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Audiences latched onto Giant (1956) as a drama theatrical experience: big enough for a group watch, specific enough to reward a second viewing once you know where it's headed. The drama and western blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Duel in the Sun
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Giant clicked for you, Duel in the Sun (1946) is a strong next stop: both trade in drama and western, with emotional whiplash that feels honest. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: The Electric Horseman
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Giant clicked for you, The Electric Horseman (1979) is a strong next stop: both trade in drama and western, with comic timing and sharper banter. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Giant?
faq.answer: Try Duel in the Sun, The Electric Horseman, Jane Eyre—each shares DNA with Giant while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Giant?
faq.answer: Giant (1956) is best described as Drama, Western, Romance. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Giant worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 7.6/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this drama lane.
